# Anedot

This is the core Anedot web application code base.

## Setup

First time? Check out the [New setup checklist in the wiki](https://github.com/anedot/anedot/wiki/New-setup-checklist)

```shell
bin/setup
```

## Development

```shell
# Run the app
bundle exec rails s
bundle exec sidekiq
bin/webpack-dev-server

# Migrate the database
bundle exec rails db:migrate

# Reset the database
bundle exec rails db:reset

# Database related commands (postgresql/redis)
brew services start postgresql   # start and register to launch on login
brew services stop postgresql
brew services restart postgresql
brew services run postgresql     # start without launching on login
```

http://anedot.me:3000

## Tests
`bundle exec rspec`

## Linter
`bundle exec rubocop`

## Database Consistency
`bundle exec rake database_consistency`

## Regenerate Swagger
`RAILS_ENV=test bundle exec rake rswag:specs:swaggerize`

## API docs
`http://anedot.me:3000/api-docs/index.html`
","metadata":{"exit_code":0,"duration_seconds":0.0}}

payload.message:
- `README.md:7` points newcomers to the internal “New setup checklist” so you follow any team-specific prerequisites (accounts, secrets, tooling) before touching the code.
- `README.md:10` shows `bin/setup`, the project’s bootstrap script; it typically installs gems, prepares databases, and ensures local configs match what the app expects.
- `README.md:16` groups the commands needed to run the app locally: `bundle exec rails s` starts the Rails web server, `bundle exec sidekiq` spins up background job processing, and `bin/webpack-dev-server` rebuilds frontend assets on the fly.
- `README.md:22` uses `bundle exec rails db:migrate` to apply schema migrations so your local database matches the current code.
- `README.md:25` calls `bundle exec rails db:reset`, dropping, recreating, migrating, and seeding the database—a clean slate if migrations or data get out of sync.
- `README.md:28-31` list Homebrew service helpers for Postgres: `start` (launch + auto-start on login), `stop`, `restart`, and `run` (one-off launch without login autostart). They ensure your database layer stays available.
- `README.md:34` documents the default local URL (`http://anedot.me:3000`) so you know where to hit the app once servers are running.
- `README.md:37` highlights `bundle exec rspec` for running the full test suite under the project’s gem versions.
- `README.md:40` reminds you to lint with `bundle exec rubocop`, enforcing Ruby style and catching common mistakes.
- `README.md:43` directs you to `bundle exec rake database_consistency`, verifying database structures and constraints match expectations.
- `README.md:46` explains how to regenerate Swagger documentation by running `RAILS_ENV=test bundle exec rake rswag:specs:swaggerize`, which executes API specs and writes fresh docs.
- `README.md:49` links to the locally served API docs at `http://anedot.me:3000/api-docs/index.html` so you can inspect or share current endpoints.

content.text:
- `README.md:7` points newcomers to the internal “New setup checklist” so you follow any team-specific prerequisites (accounts, secrets, tooling) before touching the code.
- `README.md:10` shows `bin/setup`, the project’s bootstrap script; it typically installs gems, prepares databases, and ensures local configs match what the app expects.
- `README.md:16` groups the commands needed to run the app locally: `bundle exec rails s` starts the Rails web server, `bundle exec sidekiq` spins up background job processing, and `bin/webpack-dev-server` rebuilds frontend assets on the fly.
- `README.md:22` uses `bundle exec rails db:migrate` to apply schema migrations so your local database matches the current code.
- `README.md:25` calls `bundle exec rails db:reset`, dropping, recreating, migrating, and seeding the database—a clean slate if migrations or data get out of sync.
- `README.md:28-31` list Homebrew service helpers for Postgres: `start` (launch + auto-start on login), `stop`, `restart`, and `run` (one-off launch without login autostart). They ensure your database layer stays available.
- `README.md:34` documents the default local URL (`http://anedot.me:3000`) so you know where to hit the app once servers are running.
- `README.md:37` highlights `bundle exec rspec` for running the full test suite under the project’s gem versions.
- `README.md:40` reminds you to lint with `bundle exec rubocop`, enforcing Ruby style and catching common mistakes.
- `README.md:43` directs you to `bundle exec rake database_consistency`, verifying database structures and constraints match expectations.
- `README.md:46` explains how to regenerate Swagger documentation by running `RAILS_ENV=test bundle exec rake rswag:specs:swaggerize`, which executes API specs and writes fresh docs.
- `README.md:49` links to the locally served API docs at `http://anedot.me:3000/api-docs/index.html` so you can inspect or share current endpoints.

base: &base
  adapter: postgresql
  encoding: UTF-8
  min_messages: warning
  host: localhost
  pool: 50
  username: '<%= ENV["POSTGRESQL_USERNAME"] %>'
  password: '<%= ENV["POSTGRESQL_PASSWORD"] %>'

remote: &remote
  <<: *base
  host: '<%= ENV["POSTGRESQL_ADDRESS"] %>'
  variables:
    statement_timeout: 900s
    lock_timeout: 60s

development:
  primary: &dev
    <<: *base
    database: anedot_development
    pool: 5
  primary_replica:
    <<: *dev
    replica: true

test:
  primary: &test
    <<: *base
    database: anedot_test<%= ENV['TEST_ENV_NUMBER'] %>
    min_messages: error
    pool: 5
    username: '<%= ENV["POSTGRESQL_USERNAME"] %>'
  primary_replica:
    <<: *test
    replica: true

production:
  primary:
    <<: *remote
    database: anedot_production
  primary_replica:
    <<: *remote
    host: '<%= ENV["POSTGRESQL_REPLICA_ADDRESS"] %>'
    database: anedot_production
    replica: true

staging:
  primary:
    <<: *remote
    database: anedot_production
  primary_replica:
    <<: *remote
    host: '<%= ENV["POSTGRESQL_REPLICA_ADDRESS"].presence || ENV["POSTGRESQL_ADDRESS"] %>'
    database: anedot_production
    replica: true

qa:
  primary:
    <<: *remote
    database: anedot_qa
  primary_replica:
    <<: *remote
    database: anedot_qa
    replica: true

sandbox:
  primary:
    <<: *remote
    database: anedot_sandbox
  primary_replica:
    <<: *remote
    host: '<%= ENV["POSTGRESQL_REPLICA_ADDRESS"].presence || ENV["POSTGRESQL_ADDRESS"] %>'
    database: anedot_sandbox
    replica: true
","metadata":{"exit_code":0,"duration_seconds":0.0}}

#!/usr/bin/env bash

# Set up Rails app. Run this script immediately after cloning the codebase.

# ruby version to install
RUBY_VERSION=$(cat .ruby-version)

# speed up compilation later
num_cpu=$(sysctl hw.logicalcpu | cut -d':' -f2 | tr -dc 0-9)

# colors for output
Clear='\x1b[0m'
Red='\x1b[0;31m';
Green='\x1b[0;32m';
Yellow='\x1b[0;33m';
Blue='\x1b[0;34m';
Cyan='\x1b[0;36m';

printf "%b====================================================%b\n" "$Blue" "$Clear"
printf "            %bAnedot%b Workstation Setup\n" "$Red" "$Clear"
printf "%b====================================================%b\n" "$Blue" "$Clear"
printf "\n"

# output helper functions
begin () {
  printf "\n%b[BEGIN]%b $1\n" "$Cyan" "$Clear"
}
end () {
  printf "\n%b[END]%b $1\n" "$Cyan" "$Clear"
}
indent () {
  printf "  * %s\n" "$1"
}

# install homebrew if missing
begin "Check Homebrew installation"

if [[ $(which brew) ]]; then
  indent "Homebrew already installed"
else
  begin "  * Install Homebrew"

  /bin/bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Homebrew/install/master/install.sh)"

  if [[ "$(uname -m)" = "arm64" ]]; then
    eval "$(/opt/homebrew/bin/brew shellenv)"
    printf "\n%b[NOTE]%b You may want to add the following to your ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc:\n" "$Yellow" "$Clear"
    echo "eval \"\$(/opt/homebrew/bin/brew shellenv)\""
  fi

  end "  * Install Homebrew"
fi

# homebrew completion
case $SHELL in
*/zsh)
  # assume Zsh
  zsh_completion_file="brew_zsh_completion.zsh"
  zsh_completion_src="$(brew --prefix)/Library/Contributions/$zsh_completion_file"
  zsh_completion_dest="/usr/local/share/zsh/site-functions/$zsh_completion_file"

  if [[ ! -L "$zsh_completion_dest" ]]; then
    begin "  * Install ZSH completion"

    ln -s "$zsh_completion_src" "$zsh_completion_dest"

    end "  * Install ZSH completion"
  else
    indent "ZSH completion already installed"
  fi
  ;;
*/bash)
  # assume Bash
  printf "%b[NOTE]%b Bash users:\n" "$Yellow" "$Clear"
  indent "Add to your ~/.bashrc or ~/.bash_profile (whichever you have configured to run on shell startup):"
  printf "      source %s\n" "$(brew --repository)/Library/Contributions/brew_bash_completion.sh"
  ;;
*)
  # assume something else - PRs welcome :)
esac

end "Check Homebrew installation"

# Setup Homebrew dependencies
begin "Setup Homebrew dependencies"

brew tap homebrew/bundle
brew bundle

printf "\n%b[NOTE]%b You may want to add the following to your ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc:\n" "$Yellow" "$Clear"
cat << EOF

    # enable chruby
    source $(brew --prefix)/opt/chruby/share/chruby/chruby.sh
    # automatically switch chruby
    source $(brew --prefix)/opt/chruby/share/chruby/auto.sh
    # set default ruby
    chruby $RUBY_VERSION

EOF

end "Setup Homebrew dependencies"

# Setup Ruby dependencies
begin "Setup Ruby dependencies"

# enable chruby
source $(brew --prefix)/opt/chruby/share/chruby/chruby.sh

# check if latest ruby is installed
if ! chruby | grep -q "$RUBY_VERSION"; then
  begin "  * Install Ruby $RUBY_VERSION"

  ruby-install --cleanup --jobs="$num_cpu" ruby "$RUBY_VERSION"

  # re-source chruby to find latest
  source $(brew --prefix)/opt/chruby/share/chruby/chruby.sh

  end "  * Install Ruby $RUBY_VERSION"
fi

# use latest ruby
chruby $RUBY_VERSION

bundle install --jobs "$num_cpu"

begin "Setup Node dependencies"

npm install -g n

sudo mkdir -p /usr/local/{bin,include,lib,n,share}
sudo chown -R $(whoami) /usr/local/{bin,include,lib,n,share}

n auto

yarn install

end "Setup Node dependencies"

# Add .env.development file
begin "Create .env.development file"

touch .env.development

end "Create .env.development file"

# Setup database
if [[ ! "$(pgrep postgres)" ]]; then
  # ensure postgresql is running so `db:setup` can run
  brew services start postgresql@10
fi

if [[ ! "$(pgrep redis)" ]]; then
  # ensure redis is running so `db:setup` can run
  brew services start redis
fi

begin "Setup Rails application database"

bundle exec rake db:setup

end "Setup Rails application database"

begin "Create temporary directories"

mkdir -p tmp/{pids,sockets}

end "Create temporary directories"

end "Setup Ruby dependencies"

printf "\n"
printf "%b====================================================%b\n" "$Blue" "$Clear"
printf "        %bAnedot%b Workstation Setup %bComplete!%b\n" "$Red" "$Clear" "$Green" "$Clear"
printf "%b====================================================%b\n" "$Blue" "$Clear"
printf "\n"
","metadata":{"exit_code":0,"duration_seconds":0.0}}

payload.text:
**Outlining Postgres and Rails setup steps**

I'm putting together clear, step-by-step instructions for installing Postgres 10 with Homebrew, configuring environment variables for Rails to connect to the database, and managing database setup tasks. I'll mention relevant config files like `.env.development` and `config/database.yml`, plus commands like `brew services start postgresql@10` and `bundle exec rake db:setup`. I want to help the user ensure the DB and Rails server connect smoothly and suggest verifying with commands like `psql` and checking logs if issues arise.

payload.message:
- Install Postgres and friends with `brew bundle` (or rerun `bin/setup`); the Brewfile pins `postgresql@10`, which the Rails app expects when it talks to the DB (`Brewfile`, `bin/setup`).
- Make sure the Postgres role you plan to use exists. After Homebrew installs Postgres you can run `createuser -s $(whoami)` or create a dedicated user/password inside `psql`; match whatever you choose in your environment variables.
- Populate `.env.development` with the Rails DB credentials: set `POSTGRESQL_USERNAME`, `POSTGRESQL_PASSWORD`, and optionally `POSTGRESQL_ADDRESS=localhost` so `config/database.yml` can pull them in (`config/database.yml:7-13`).
- Start the database service with `brew services start postgresql@10`; use `brew services list` to confirm it’s running, and repeat the same for Redis if Sidekiq needs it.
- Create and migrate the schema once Postgres is running: `bundle exec rake db:setup` (or `rails db:create db:migrate`). That builds `anedot_development`/`anedot_test` and loads the schema so Rails can connect cleanly at boot.
- Launch the stack in this order to pick up the DB connection: Postgres service, Redis, then `bundle exec rails s`; the server will use those env vars to open a connection and should stop complaining about missing databases.

Next step if anything still fails: check `log/development.log` for connection errors or try `psql -d anedot_development` as the same user to confirm the credentials work.

summary.text:
**Outlining Postgres and Rails setup steps**

I'm putting together clear, step-by-step instructions for installing Postgres 10 with Homebrew, configuring environment variables for Rails to connect to the database, and managing database setup tasks. I'll mention relevant config files like `.env.development` and `config/database.yml`, plus commands like `brew services start postgresql@10` and `bundle exec rake db:setup`. I want to help the user ensure the DB and Rails server connect smoothly and suggest verifying with commands like `psql` and checking logs if issues arise.

content.text:
- Install Postgres and friends with `brew bundle` (or rerun `bin/setup`); the Brewfile pins `postgresql@10`, which the Rails app expects when it talks to the DB (`Brewfile`, `bin/setup`).
- Make sure the Postgres role you plan to use exists. After Homebrew installs Postgres you can run `createuser -s $(whoami)` or create a dedicated user/password inside `psql`; match whatever you choose in your environment variables.
- Populate `.env.development` with the Rails DB credentials: set `POSTGRESQL_USERNAME`, `POSTGRESQL_PASSWORD`, and optionally `POSTGRESQL_ADDRESS=localhost` so `config/database.yml` can pull them in (`config/database.yml:7-13`).
- Start the database service with `brew services start postgresql@10`; use `brew services list` to confirm it’s running, and repeat the same for Redis if Sidekiq needs it.
- Create and migrate the schema once Postgres is running: `bundle exec rake db:setup` (or `rails db:create db:migrate`). That builds `anedot_development`/`anedot_test` and loads the schema so Rails can connect cleanly at boot.
- Launch the stack in this order to pick up the DB connection: Postgres service, Redis, then `bundle exec rails s`; the server will use those env vars to open a connection and should stop complaining about missing databases.

Next step if anything still fails: check `log/development.log` for connection errors or try `psql -d anedot_development` as the same user to confirm the credentials work.
